You easily want to drive away 180 of them. I mean, you want the 20 around you to be ready able and willing to give you money. You have to sacrifice, in some cases, the total satisfaction of the host who gave you the speaking opportunity. They may not be totally thrilled with what it is that you do up there. And for the most part, it doesn't matter. You have to be very purposeful and very strategic about the speech, just as you would be about everything else we've talked about today. And that means every word has to move them towards separation and the ones who are ready able and willing to give you money to surface and come up and chase you down and try and give you money. Every word, every story, every, if you do use humor, every piece of humor, every example you show. So for example, let's say you're speaking to a group of business owners, none of whom are dentists. Then showing them a dentist example or talking about a dentist, no matter how instructive that example may be and how relevant it is to what you teach, you shouldn't do it. It's a waste. None of them will relate to it because they're not dentists. And so you just sucked up three minutes or five minutes or eight minutes of time and space doing something that does not move them further to where you want them to be. If you have a pet story you like to tell about your kid or your dog or your boating trip or whatever, if it doesn't advance the movement of the segment of the audience who can and will give you money, scrap the story. All story that's not near as good, but that serves the strategic purpose of what it is that you are there to do. If you want to speak as a hobby, that's one thing. But if you want to speak to drive this business, then the speech has to be built to drive this business every minute of it, every word of it, every item of it. Most, again, most speakers are not that strategic about what they do. They don't think about who they're trying to attract, who they're trying to repel, what they may say that will resonate with the right people, how to get them to come to them, how overt or unoverte to be. And so they build a speech sort of like, what do I want to talk about, or what will the audience like, or what will be funny, and they go give it. And sometimes people like it, but it doesn't lead anywhere. And you need it to lead somewhere. I'm a friend who not long ago had his first and only speaking opportunity to promote his business, and it was a very good opportunity. And he went and he spoke about a thousand people there. And he should have come out of there with half a million dollars. Immediately, I'm not talking about books and tapes in the back of the room, but I'm talking about business. And hardly anything has happened from it. And he said, I can't understand it. You know, I was brilliant. I was, you know, mesmerizing. I shared tremendous information. They laughed. They cried. They clapped. They all gathered around me afterwards. Yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah. I said, did anybody record it? Yeah, they record it. Sorry. Get the recording transcribed. Send me the transcription. Well, the problem's obvious. 90 minutes, the guy never mentioned what he does. Never. Not once. So how would you know you're supposed to come hire him? I mean, he didn't take it away. He didn't put it out. He didn't do anything. He gave a great speech and went home. And it's perplexed at the fact that it didn't turn into any money. You got to like point him in the direction you want him to go. The last thing on this list, and we'll take a few questions, and then we'll ultimately break for dinner. So very shortly, I'm just going to do open Q&A. So if any of you want to play, you might want to come up to the table. The last thing on this list will, for some of you, be the worst piece of news you've had all day. But here it is anyway. The truth is, you got to write. And you're going to have to get good at that, too. Now, a lot of my clients, they act as if putting on a newsletter once a month is like giving birth to triplet elephants. And...
